checks.a_analysis: I have compared the output text against the source PDF. All paragraphs, headings, and list items in the output have a direct basis in the source document. No fabricated body text or invented sections were found.
The source document does not contain explicit contact information like phone numbers or email addresses. The output correctly reflects the textual content without adding any invented contact details.

The source document consists of 14 pages including a title page, table of contents, technical specifications, two appendices containing several tables, and a references page. All these sections and pages are fully represented in the output JSON structure.

I counted the rows in all tables in both the source and the output. For example, the 'Appendix A - Fields' table is split across pages 4-8 in the source with a total of 45 data rows; the output captures all 45 rows across five corresponding table elements. All other tables in Appendix B also match their source counterparts in row count.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I reviewed the text content of each section (Preface, Introduction, Definitions, Requirements, etc.) and found that the output preserves the full substance of the source text. No significant omissions of paragraphs or key sentences were detected.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The output text accurately reflects the source content. Minor differences such as whitespace normalization or punctuation are within acceptable limits. The technical data in the tables (types, lengths, domains, descriptions) is faithful to the source.

checks.a_analysis: An examination of the text content across all sections shows that paragraphs and headings are unique and correspond to their single occurrences in the source document. While table headers are repeated across section splits (e.g., Appendix A and B), this is a standard normalization for handling page breaks in long tables and does not constitute a defect. Watermark images ('DRAFT') are extracted as separate objects on each page, which reflects the source's layout.

The heading hierarchy is well-maintained. The document title is H1, major sections like 'Contents' and 'AddressNC Data Content Standard' are H1 or H2, and sub-sections follow a logical H2 to H3 progression. For instance, in section 3, the parent 'General Data Requirements' is H2, and its children like 'Coordinate System' and 'Metadata Standards' are correctly assigned H3.

The tables, particularly in Appendix A and Appendix B, are complex and span multiple pages. The pipeline has successfully preserved the column count (7 for Appendix A, 3 for Place Types) across all page-specific chunks. Header rows are correctly identified and applied to each table segment. The data within the cells is correctly aligned with the appropriate headers.
The reading order follows the logical flow of the source PDF, starting with the cover page, moving through the table of contents, and then into the main text and appendices.

There are two minor defects in list conversion. First, the Table of Contents on page 2 contains a nested structure (e.g., 'Preface' and 'Coordinate System' are sub-items), but the output represents this as a flat unordered list, losing the hierarchical relationship. Second, the References section on page 14 is a numbered list (1-8) in the source, but the output converts it to an unordered list, losing the explicit numbering sequence.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 2 Table of Contents shows 'Preface' and 'Introduction' indented under 'AddressNC Data Content Standard'. Page 14 shows a numbered list (1 through 8).
checks.b_evidence_output: The list in section 1 (index 1) is a flat list. The list in section 13 (index 1) has 'list_type': 'unordered'.

checks.a_analysis: No external URLs are present in the text of the source PDF. Internal cross-references (superscripts) are preserved as text, and no fabricated links were injected.

checks.a_analysis: The rendered HTML follows the organizational structure of the source PDF. The sequence of sections (Preface, Introduction, Definition, General Data Requirements, Appendix A, Appendix B, References) matches the source. The layout correctly transitions from standard text paragraphs to complex data tables in the appendices. The responsive HTML design appropriately handles the transition from a fixed-page PDF format to a continuous web document.

The tables in Appendix A (Fields) and Appendix B (Domains) are correctly rendered as structured tables in the HTML. Column headers are visually distinct, and the relationship between rows and columns is maintained. The multi-page tables from the PDF (e.g., the Place Type domain table spanning pages 9-13) are unified into logical table structures in the HTML, preserving readability and data integrity. No cell overlapping or misalignment was observed.

The primary meaningful image from the source PDF—the map of North Carolina on the cover page—is present and correctly rendered in the HTML output. The 'DRAFT' watermarks present in the source are absent, which is consistent with the instructions as an intentional accessibility improvement for decorative elements.

A thorough visual scan of the rendered HTML confirms that body content is not duplicated. Section headers and table rows appear exactly once in the document flow. While titles appear in the table of contents and the body, this is identified as an expected accessibility feature rather than a content duplication defect.
